rulings.Case Title: LAURA ALBERT ET AL VS. INTERNATIONAL HIGH SCHOOL, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Trevor Knoop Motion For Summary Adjudication Notice And Motion For Summary Adjudication (2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 24, 2019, Line 4, Plaintiff Trevor Knoop Motion For Summary Adjudication Notice And Motion For Summary Adjudication (2 Of 2) This Court is bound by Paramount Petroleum Corp. (See Auto Equity Sales, Inc. v. Superior Court (1962) 57 Cal.2d 450, 455.) The Court notes that the only authority Plaintiff cites to justify his motion for partial summary adjudication is an unpublished Court of Appeal opinion, which is improper. (Cal. R. Ct. 8.1115(a) ["an opinion of a California Court of Appeal . . . that is not certified for publication must not be cited or relied on by a court or a party in any other action."]; see People v. Williams (2009) 176 Cal.App.4th 1521, 1529 ["We realize that depublished and unpublished decisions are now as readily available as published cases, thanks to the Internet and technologically savvy legal research programs. That does not give counsel an excuse to ignore the rules of court. Indeed, persistent use of unpublished authority may be cause for sanctions."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LAURA ALBERT ET AL VS. INTERNATIONAL HIGH SCHOOL, ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Notice And Motion For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 24, 2019, Line 4 Motion For Summary Adjudication (1 of 2) Plaintiff Trevor Knoop's motion for summary adjudication of liability on the sixth cause of action for battery and the seventh cause of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress as to Defendants Asher Holman and Emmanuel Al-Thani is denied, on several independent grounds. First, the motion for summary adjudication was set for hearing less than 30 days before the date of trial without a prior court order establishing good cause. (Code Civ. Proc. §§ 437c(a)(2),(3); see Robinson v. Woods (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1258, 1268 ["Unless and until the trial court found good cause, the notice of the hearing was invalid."].) Second, the motion is unsupported by a separate statement of material facts in proper form. (Code Civ. Proc. § 437c(b)(1); Cal. R. Ct. 3.1350(d).) Third, and most importantly, the motion is denied on the ground that it fails to dispose of an entire cause of action. A motion for summary adjudication shall be granted only if it completely disposes of a cause of action, an affirmative defense, a claim for damages, or an issue of duty." (Code Civ. Proc § 437c(f)(1).) Plaintiff's unsupported assertion in his moving papers that "[s]ummary adjudication can be granted as to the issue of liability, leaving for the trial the amount of damages," (Mot. at 3), is incorrect. (Paramount Petroleum Corp. v. Superior Court (2014) 227 Cal.App.4th 226, 241 ["May a plaintiff seek summary adjudication of liability only leaving the resolution of damages to a later trial? The statutory language mandates the question be answered in the negative. A plaintiff can obtain summary adjudication of a cause of action only by proving 'each element of the cause of action entitling the party to judgment on that cause of action.' (Code Civ. Proc., § 437c, subd. (p)(1).)"].) Damages and causation are elements of Plaintiff's causes of action for battery and intentional infliction of emotional distress. (See Brown v. Ransweiler (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 516, 527 [elements of civil battery include that "the harmful or offensive contact caused injury, damage, loss or harm to plaintiff"]; Light v. Department of Parks and Recreation (2017) 14 Cal.App.5th 75, 101 [elements of intentional infliction of emotional distress include the plaintiff's suffering severe or extreme emotional distress and actual and proximate causation of the emotional distress by the defendant's outrageous conduct]; CACI No. 1604 [definition of severe emotional distress].) Plaintiff could have sought leave to move for summary adjudication of a legal issue that did not completely dispose of a cause of action, based on the parties' stipulation that the motion would further the interest of judicial economy by decreasing trial time or significantly increasing the likelihood of settlement. (Code Civ. Proc. § 437c(t).) Having failed to do so, summary adjudication is unavailable. In any event, there appear to be substantial disputed issues of material fact bearing on the causation and extent of Plaintiff's asserted emotional distress and other damages that will be the subject of contested expert testimony, including the results of the independent medical examination conducted on Plaintiff. (Breen Decl. 3-5 & Ex. 2.) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LIAN TONG, LLC VS. BING WU CONSTRUCTION COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Bing Wu, Kai Lei'S Motion For Attorneys Fees Against Lian Tong, Llc
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 24, 2019, Line 16, Defendants Bing Wu, Kai Lei'S Motion For Attorneys Fees Against Lian Tong, Llc Defendants and Cross-Complainants Bing Wu and Kai Ming Lei f/d/b/a Bing Wu Construction Company's motion for attorneys' fees against Plaintiff and Cross-Defendant Lian Tong, LLC is denied. The motion is brought under Code of Civil Procedure Section 425.16(c)(1), which provides that if the court finds that a special motion to strike is "frivolous or is solely intended to cause unnecessary delay, the court shall award costs and reasonable attorney's fees to a plaintiff [here, a cross-complainant] prevailing on the motion, pursuant to Section 128.5." Defendants' motion is timely. (Carpenter v. Jack in the Box Corp. (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 454, 462-468 [holding that a motion for attorney fees by the prevailing plaintiff on a special motion to strike is timely under Rule 3.1702 of the California Rules of Court if filed before entry of final judgment at the conclusion of the lawsuit]. However, as Plaintiff correctly observes, in opposing its special motion to strike the first amended cross-complaint, Defendants specifically requested the Court to find that the motion was frivolous or was solely intended to cause unnecessary delay. In its March 18, 2019 order denying Plaintiff's motion, the Court made no such finding. Accordingly, the instant motion amounts to an untimely and improper motion for reconsideration. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1008(a).) The motion also fails on the merits. The Court denied Plaintiff's motion on the ground that it was untimely. As Defendants properly recognize, however, "the fact that a special motion to strike was filed untimely, standing alone, cannot support a finding that the motion is frivolous or solely intended to cause unnecessary delay." (Chitsazzadeh v. Kramer & Kaslow (2011) 199 Cal.App.4th 676, 684.) Rather, "whether the sole purpose of the motion is to harass an opposing party or whether the motion is solely intended to cause unnecessary delay also depends on either the objective merits of the motion or some other indication of the moving defendant's subjective motivation and cannot be inferred from the fact that the motion was untimely." (Id.) Here, Defendants do not claim that Plaintiff's subjective motivation in bringing the motion can be inferred from the objective merits (or lack thereof) of the motion. Nor could they because, as Plaintiff points out, on May 14, 2019, this Court granted Plaintiff's motion for judgment on the pleadings as to all six causes of action of the first amended cross-complaint, two of them without leave to amend. Defendants argue that Plaintiffs' subjective motion to cause unnecessary delay can be inferred from Plaintiff's having sought and obtained a continuance of the trial date on the basis of the pendency of its special motion to strike. However, Defendants have not shown that was the only basis for Plaintiff's request for a continuance, which was also based on the status of the pleadings and of discovery, and therefore have not shown that the anti-SLAPP motion was "solely intended to cause unnecessary delay." Accordingly, the motion is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: TOUR-SARKISSIAN LAW OFFICES LLP VS. LAMA ASHKAR ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Court'S Own Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Plaintiff'S Ex Parte Application For Entry Of Judgment Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure 664.6
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 24, 2019, Line 5, Court'S Own Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Plaintiff'S Ex Parte Application For Entry Of Judgment Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure 664.6 The Court vacates its June 7, 2019 order granting the ex parte application of Plaintiff Tour-Sarkissian Law Offices, LLP for entry of judgment pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure Section 664.6 in the amount of $57,408.22, and grants Plaintiff's motion for entry of judgment in the stipulated settlement amount of $10,000.00. As Plaintiff evidently was aware when it entered into the Settlement Agreement and Release of Claims, the parties' settlement sought to impose an unenforceable penalty within the meaning of Greentree Financial Group, Inc. v. Execute Sports, Inc. (2008) 163 Cal.App.4th 495. (See also, e.g., Purcell v. Schweitzer (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 969, 974-976.) Under the circumstances, entry of judgment in the stipulated amount is the appropriate remedy. (See Vivatech International, Inc. v. Sporn (2017) 16 Cal.App.5th 796, 800, 815 [reversing and remanding for trial court to grant motion and enter a new judgment for the settlement amount, plus costs]; Greentree Financial Group, Inc., 163 Cal.App.4th at 498, 503 [same].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TERESITA SANTOS WILLIAMS VS. ALBERTO A. SANTOS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants' Motion To Enforce Settlement. Ex Parte Application To Serve As Moving Papers.
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 24, 2019, Line 15, Defendants' Motion To Enforce Settlement. Ex Parte Application To Serve As Moving Papers. Defendants Alberto A. Santos and Nely Santos's motion to enforce settlement is granted. On February 11, 2019, the parties entered into a partly handwritten Stipulation for Settlement, signed by all parties and counsel, which had been prepared on February 8, 2019 during mediation with a neutral bench officer at ADR Services. The signed Stipulation for Settlement contains a provision whereby the court reserved jursidiction to enforce the terms and conditions of the settlement pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure Section 664.6. The Stipulation for Settlement recited that it was "intended to be binding and enforceable" effective February 11, 2019 and "represents the final agreement between the parties to this dispute." Plaintiff Teresita Santos Williams states that "[s]oon after" signing the Stipulation for Settlement, she wanted to "rescind" the settlement on the ground that Defendant Alberto Santos purportedly told her at the courthouse "that [she] should sign the settlement because he did not want to go to trial and we would have a different settlement." (Williams Decl. 3, 7.) In a letter she sent to her attorney on May 31, 2019, she also asserted that at the time she signed the Stipulation for Settlement, she was "under stress, duress, confused, even coerced," and claimed that it "seems like I was misled, deceived and manipulated" by Santos. However, there are no facts before the Court to support Plaintiff's contention, conveyed solely to her attorney in a hearsay letter, that her consent was obtained by duress or fraud. Moreover, it is undisputed that the agreement was entered into after a mediation presided over by a retired judge and a settlement conference presided over by a judge pro tem, and that when Ms. Williams signed the Stipulation for Settlement, she was represented by counsel. A party's change of heart after agreeing to a settlement is not a valid ground for avoiding a contract. Nor is a party's subjective belief that she will not be bound by a signed settlement agreement. (See Mission Viejo Emergency Medical Associates v. Beta Healthcare Group (2011) 197 Cal.App.4th 1146, 1154-1155 ["In California, we adhere to the objective theory of contract law. Under that theory, '[i]t is the objective intent, as evidenced by the words of the contract, rather than the subjective intent of one of the parties, that controls interpretation.'"].) Contrary to Plaintiffs' argument, Defendants are not seeking to have the Court "create material terms to the settlement," but only to enforce it according to its terms. Nor does the parties' failure to sign the formal settlement documentation that was later prepared present a bar to its enforcement. (Blix Street Records, Inc. v. Cassidy (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 39, 48 ["When parties agree that an agreement be binding, the fact that a more formal agreement must be prepared and executed does not alter the validity of the agreement."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: FRANCISCO MARTINEZ-VILLANUEVA VS. ALLIANCE CAB COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Response To Request For Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 24, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF FRANCISCO MARTINEZ-VILLANUEVA'S Motion To Compel Response To Request For Production Of Documents. Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is unopposed and will be granted with sanctions for discovery abuse totaling $500 for this and the two companion motions awarded against defendant Rosenthall and his counsel of record. If this tentative ruling is unopposed, no appearance is required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jameshfleming@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email a copy of it to jameshfleming@gmail.com even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD-STARTX FUND, LLC ET AL VS. MEDWHAT.COM INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendants Responses To First Sets Of Discovery Requests, And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 24, 2019, Line 9, PLAINTIFF STANFORD-STARTX FUND, LLC Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendants Responses To First Sets Of Discovery Requests, And For Sanctions Hearing required. In a "Notice" filed with the Court on June 19, 2019, Defendant Arturo Devesa represents that "Defendant has already responded, after spending weeks doing so, to Discovery Interrogatories on June 18, 2019." The parties shall be prepared at the hearing to address the following issues: 1. Has Mr. Devesa served verified answers, without objections, to the outstanding form interrogatories and special interrogatories accurate? 2. Has Mr. Devesa also produced documents and served verified responses, without objections, to the Fund's requests for production of documents? 3. Has Mr. Devesa served verified responses to the Fund's requests for admissions? When were his responses served? (See Code Civ. Proc. § 2033.280(c) [court shall enter order that matters be deemed admitted and for monetary sanctions unless "the party to whom the requests for admission have been directed has served, before the hearing on the motion, a proposed response to the requests for admission that is in substantial compliance with Section 2033.220." (emphasis added)] 4. In the event that Defendant Medwhat.com, Inc. has not responded to the outstanding discovery requests, may this Court compel it to provide discovery responses, given that it is currently unrepresented by counsel and may not represent itself in the proceedings before the Court? 5. If the Court determines that an award of sanctions is appropriate solely against Mr. Devesa, how (if at all) should the total amount of sanctions sought by Plaintiff be allocated as between Mr. Devesa and Medwhat.com, Inc.? 6. When is it anticipated that Medwhat.com, Inc. will retain counsel to represent it? =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JACLYN MCGLYNN VS. 634 POWELL I5, LLC
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses By Plaintiffs
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 24, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT 634 POWELL I5, LLC, YAT-PANG AU, VERITAS INVESTMENTS, INC., GREENTREE PROPERTY MANAGEMENT, INC.'S Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses By Plaintiffs.(part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Counsel shall appear and be prepared to address the following topics: 1. Whether there is authority that omission of information from a Separate Statements of moving party mandate denial of the motion to compel. 2. Were responsive separate statements required and if so whether there is justification for failure to file responsive Separate Statements as to every plaintiff. If not, what are the consequences? 3. Whether plaintiffs have a right of associational privacy as to contacts with the Housing Rights Committee of San Francisco ("HRC") under the holdings of Britt v. Superior Court and related cases. In that connection, to what extent does HRC resemble the organizations addressed in Britt and related cases? 4. Whether there has been, as Britt acknowledges, a partial waiver of associational rights by the filing of this lawsuit by the filing of this lawsuit and to what extent? 5. Does the standard of direct relevance apply to discovery as to HRC and how may that standard be applied in this instance? 6. If less than all requested HRC discovery is allowed, can defendants' discovery rights can be adequately protected by a privilege log covering HRC-related items which are redacted or withheld from discovery? 7. In the event a privilege log is ordered, what factual information is necessary for other parties to evaluate the merits of the claim of privilege? 8. Is it permissible to respond to discovery by an omnibus statement which is repeatedly used for multiple discovery responses? 9. Does the common interest doctrine apply to communications between plaintiffs outside the presence of counsel but including some reference to consultation with counsel? 10. If so, can defendants' discovery rights can be adequately protected by a privilege log including common interest items which are redacted or withheld from discovery as including reference to consultation with counsel? 11. In the event such a privilege log is ordered, what factual information necessary for other parties to evaluate the merits of the claim of privilege? 12. Should sanctions for discovery abuse be awarded for or against any party or counsel? 13. Such other topics as the parties deem necessary for an appropriate resolution of this discovery dispute.(end of part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ADA JACKSON VS. SWISSPORT, SA, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 24, 2019, Line 12, DEFENDANT SWISSPORT SA, LLC, SWISSPORT USA, INC. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration The case is stayed in its entirety pending the California Supreme Court's forthcoming decision in ZB, N.A. v. Superior Court (Lawson), Case No. S246711. (See Freiberg v. City of Mission Viejo (1995) 33 Cal.App.4th 1484, 1489 ["Trial courts generally have the inherent power to stay proceedings in the interests of justice and to promote judicial efficiency."].) The threshold issues presented by this motion to compel arbitration are (1) how unpaid wages recoverable under Labor Code section 558 brought as part of a PAGA action are properly allocated between the plaintiff and the State Labor and Workforce Development Agency (LWDA) and (2) how that allocation impacts the arbitrability of section 558 claims brought as part of a PAGA action. California's appellate courts are split on this question. (Compare Zakaryan v. The Men's Wearhouse, Inc. (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 659, 674-676 [section 558 claim not arbitrable as part of PAGA action, but 75% of the recovery of unpaid wages must be paid to LWDA]; Lawson v. ZB, N.A. (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 705, 721, 724-725, review granted [section 558 claims not arbitrable and plaintiff is entitled to 100% of unpaid wages recovered]; and Esparza v. KS Industries, L.P. (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 1228, 1246, review denied [section 558 claim is a private claim for unpaid wages that should be split from the PAGA action and arbitrated].) The Court finds good cause to await guidance from the Supreme Court before deciding this motion. Oral argument was held on June 5, 2019, and the Court has requested supplemental briefs be filed on June 20 and June 27, 2019, at which time the matter will be resubmitted. A final decision should follow within 90 days. After the Supreme Court issues its ruling, the parties should contact this Court to set a new hearing date on the motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BRYANT FU VS. MEI LING FANG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Declaring Bryant Fu A Vexatious Litigant, For Security, And For Prefiling Order
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 24, 2019, Line 13, DEFENDANT WILLIAM MORA's Motion For An Order Declaring Bryant Fu A Vexatious Litigant, For Security, And For Prefiling Order Defendant William Mora's motion for an order declaring Plaintiff Bryant Fu a vexatious litigant, for security, and for prefiling order is denied. Defendant has not shown that Plaintiff is a vexatious litigant within the meaning of Civil Code Section 391(b)(3). Further, because the Court has sustained the demurrer to the first amended complaint without leave to amend, it denies Defendant's request that it require Plaintiff to furnish security under Code of Civil Procedure Section 391.1, a provision which only applies to "pending" litigation "at any time until final judgment is entered." Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: BRYANT FU VS. MEI LING FANG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, June 24, 2019, Line 13, DEFENDANT WILLIAM MORA DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendant William Mora's demurrer to the first amended complaint is sustained, as to Mr. Mora only, without leave to amend. Plaintiff alleges that in June 2014, he obtained a sanctions award against a non-party, Dennis Yan, for taking a frivolous appeal to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. (Compl. 18.) He alleges further that Yan fraudulently transferred assets to third parties, including Legal Recovery, LLC, to avoid paying that award. (Id. 21-23, 26.) The only charging allegations against Mr. Mora in the first amended complaint are that he is the agent for service of process of Legal Recovery, and is not a California resident, and that he has "actively rendered Legal Recovery unable to be served with process to frustrate [Plaintiff] and other creditors' attempts to recover assets Yan has hidden behind Legal Recovery." (Id. 29, 30.) Taking these allegations as true, as required on demurrer, they do not state a cause of action against Mora for fraud or conspiracy to defraud. Liberally construing the first amended complaint, Plaintiff (who is representing himself in propria persona) appears to be attempting to state a cause of action against Mora for aiding and abetting a fraudulent transfer in violation of the former Uniform Fraudulent Transfer Act, Civ. Code §3439 et seq., which applied until January 1, 2016, when it was superseded by the Uniform Voidable Transactions Act with respect to transfers made on or after the effective date. (Civ. Code § 3439.14(a).) However, Mora is not alleged to be the transferee of property fraudulently transferred by Yan, but only the agent for service of process for the entity to which the property was transferred, and he is not alleged to have committed any act in furtherance of the alleged conspiracy to defraud Plaintiff or to conceal Yan's assets from him. Further, Plaintiff alleges that the fraudulent transfer to Legal Recovery occurred "shortly after Plaintiff obtained his sanctions award against Yan" (id. 26) in June 2014, and that he discovered Yan's fraudulent use of Legal Recovery in March 2017. (Id. 22-24, 26.) As a result, any claim for fraudulent transfer would be time-barred, because the original complaint was filed on December 27, 2018, more than four years after the alleged fraudulent transfer (and more than one year after Plaintiff allegedly discovered Yan's use of Legal Recovery). (Civ. Code § 3439.09 [a cause of action with respect to a fraudulent transfer is "extinguished" unless action is brought "within four years after the transfer was made or the obligation was incurred or, if later, within one year after the transfer or obligation was or could reasonably have been discovered by the claimant"].) The demurrer to the third cause of action is sustained because there is no independent cause of action for injunctive relief. Because Plaintiff has not shown any reasonable possibility that he can amend the complaint to state a cause of action, the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
